title: The infinitesimal environmental dust as a photonic bath at infinity
authors: Gaomin Tang, Yuhua Ren, Jian-Sheng Wang
abstract: In far-field thermal radiation, electromagnetic waves emitted by an object propagate to infinity, requiring the far region to be modeled as an effective thermal bath. This bath was proposed as infinitesimal environmental "dust", but explicit calculations with such distributed dust involve integrals over infinite space that are difficult to evaluate. In this work, we map this environmental dust to a photonic bath at infinity within the nonequilibrium photonic Green's function formalism. By explicitly evaluating the spatial integral over the dust, we show that its contribution reduces to a simple local self-energy, for which we derive analytical expressions for both three-dimensional objects and planar systems. We further demonstrate that the bath behaves as a black body and clarify its role in far-field thermal radiation. An alternative derivation based on the surface Green's function framework is also provided in Appendix B, demonstrating the theoretical consistency of the results without invoking the dust model. The photonic bath at infinity provides a convenient framework for both analytical and numerical calculations in far-field thermal radiation.
summary: This paper simplifies the complex problem of calculating far-field thermal radiation by replacing an infinite volume of 'environmental dust' with a simple, local photonic bath at infinity. The authors derive analytical expressions for this bath in both 3D and 2D geometries, demonstrating that it correctly recovers black-body radiation properties. This new formalism provides a much more efficient way to perform both analytical and numerical calculations in radiative heat transfer.
main_problem: The difficulty of modeling far-field thermal radiation due to the computational complexity of integrating electromagnetic wave propagation over infinite space using the 'environmental dust' model.
main_result: The authors successfully map the distributed environmental dust to a simplified 'photonic bath at infinity' represented by a local self-energy, proving it behaves as a black body and providing analytical expressions for 3D and 2D systems.
method: The study utilizes the nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism, specifically the Keldysh Green's function, and employs a surface Green's function framework for verification.
model_or_system: A material object (either a 3D body or a 2D planar system) at temperature T1 surrounded by an environment at temperature T0, modeled as a medium with infinitesimal absorption (dust).
key_observables: Radiative energy and momentum transfer, Casimir force, far-field heat, momentum, and angular-momentum radiation, and energy density.
important_parameters: Temperatures T1 and T0, frequency omega, infinitesimal absorption parameter eta, and the vacuum permittivity and permeability.
main_assumptions: The model assumes infinitesimal absorption (eta to 0+), time-translational invariance, and a linear-response regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic comparison between the traditional distributed dust model and the proposed effective photonic bath at infinity model for both 3D and 2D systems; Figure 2 shows a discretized lattice model used for the surface Green's function derivation.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of far-field radiation modeling, introduces the mapping of dust to a photonic bath using NEGF, derives the effective self-energy and black-body behavior for 3D and 2D systems, and concludes with an alternative derivation using a surface Green's function framework and lattice renormalization.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful new computational framework for calculating far-field thermal radiation and Casimir forces, which is highly relevant for researchers working on open quantum systems and quantum optics in the presence of thermal environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of thermal radiation from (a) a three-dimensional object of finite size and (b) a semi-infinite object filling the region of z < 0 to the surrounding environment. Both objects are maintained at temperature T1, while the surrounding environment is at temper- ature T0. The left panels depict that the environment extending to infinity is modeled as infinitesimal “dust” with homogeneous dielec- tric properties modeled by self-energy Πr 0 (gray shading). The right panels show the equivalent theoretical representation in which the ef- fect of the distributed dust is modeled as a photonic bath at infinity with effective self-energy Πr ∞.
